title: Getting Around
subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Fiji.

items.label: Nadi International Hub
items.text: Nadi International Airport (NAN) on Viti Levu is Fiji's main gateway. Most international flights arrive here; Suva and Nausori have smaller regional airports. Taxis and hotel shuttles queue outside arrivals, or arrange transport ahead to avoid inflated on-spot rates.

items.label: Inter-island Flights
items.text: Fiji Airways and Pacific Sun operate domestic routes between major islands (Suva, Nausori, Labasa). Book flights in advance during cyclone season (November–April) as weather delays are common.
Airport taxis have no meters; agree on the fare before entering. Official taxi stands are safer than unmarked vehicles; expect around FJD 50–70 (USD 23–32) to Nadi town.

items.label: Patterson Brothers & Bligh Water
items.text: Patterson Brothers Shipping operates car and passenger ferries between Viti Levu and outer islands (Vanua Levu, Taveuni). The Bligh Water crossing is scenic but can be rough in bad weather.

items.label: Fast Catamaran Services
items.text: Seacoast Airways and local speedboat operators run frequent routes between resort islands and main hubs. Book through your accommodation or ask at the waterfront; departures often depend on passenger numbers.
Ferry schedules change with monsoon and cyclone seasons. Confirm departure times the day before and arrive 30 minutes early; no refunds for missed sailings due to weather.

items.label: Fiji's Main Bus Networks
items.text: Sunbeam, Pacific Transport, and local operators run frequent minibuses and coaches on main routes across Viti Levu and between towns. Routes radiate from Suva and Nadi; journey times are slow due to multiple stops.

items.label: Fares & Payment
items.text: Fares are extremely cheap (FJD 1–5 for short hops, up to FJD 15–20 for inter-town routes). Pay the driver in cash; no apps or passes exist for most routes.

items.label: Crowding & Safety
items.text: Minibuses are often packed beyond comfort; pickpockets work crowded routes, especially during rush hours. Keep valuables secure and avoid traveling alone late at night.

items.label: Long-Distance Coaches
items.text: Sunbeam and Pacific Transport run overnight coaches from Suva to Nadi and regional towns; book ahead at terminals or through hotels for reserved seats.

items.label: Metered Taxis in Towns
items.text: Metered yellow taxis operate in Suva and Nadi; insist the driver use the meter or agree on the fare first. Rates are roughly FJD 2–3 per km.

items.label: No Major Ride Apps
items.text: Uber does not operate in Fiji. Ride-sharing is limited; rely on hotel concierge to call registered taxis or use local apps like Uber's former alternatives where available.

items.label: Shared Taxis & Carriers
items.text: Shared minibus taxis (carriers) run fixed routes for cheap fares; they depart when full rather than on schedule, and stops are informal along the roadside.
Unmetered taxis and unmarked vehicles may overcharge tourists. Use official taxi stands at hotels, restaurants, or major landmarks; avoid hailing on dark streets at night.

items.label: License & Requirements
items.text: You must hold a valid international driving permit (or home license with IDP) to rent a car in Fiji. Driving is on the left side of the road.

items.label: Road Conditions
items.text: Roads outside main towns are potholed, poorly lit, and narrow; many are unsealed. During wet season (November–April), flooding and landslides close roads unexpectedly.

items.label: Rental Operators
items.text: Hertz, Avis, Budget, and local firms (Thrifty Fiji, Happy Tours) rent vehicles at airports and in town. Daily rates start around FJD 60–100 (USD 27–46); fuel is expensive.

items.label: Hazards & Fines
items.text: Speed limits are 50 km/h in towns and 80 km/h on highways; police conduct random breath tests for alcohol (legal limit is 0.05%). Fines for speeding or seatbelt violations are FJD 50–300.

items.label: Parking & Security
items.text: Park in designated lots or hotel grounds; leave nothing visible in the car. Theft from vehicles is common in Suva and Nadi, especially after dark.

items.label: No Unified Ticketing System
items.text: Fiji has no integrated transit pass or card system. Each operator (buses, ferries, taxis) is independent; buy tickets individually at terminals, on board, or through booking apps.

items.label: Advance Ferry & Flight Bookings
items.text: Book long-distance ferries and domestic flights 1–2 days ahead through tour operators, hotels, or online (Fiji Airways, Patterson Brothers websites). Last-minute bookings are expensive or unavailable.

items.label: Cash Is King
items.text: Most buses, minivans, and informal taxis accept only cash (Fiji dollars). ATMs are widely available in towns and resorts; cards work in shops and hotels.
Refund and cancellation policies vary; ferries and buses rarely refund for no-shows. Travel insurance covering transport delays is recommended during cyclone season.

items.label: Main SIM Providers
items.text: Vodafone Fiji and bmobile-vodafone are the dominant mobile networks. Both offer prepaid SIM cards at airports, shops, and resorts; a basic SIM costs FJD 10–20 with credit.

items.label: Data & Roaming Plans
items.text: Prepaid data packages are affordable (FJD 10 for 1 GB, FJD 30 for 5 GB). International roaming is expensive; buy a local SIM for better rates if staying beyond a few days.

items.label: Coverage Gaps
items.text: Mobile signal is poor or absent in remote villages and outer islands. WiFi is common at resorts, hotels, and urban cafés but can be slow or unreliable.

items.label: Staying Connected for Transport
items.text: Reliable connectivity helps confirm ferry schedules, book taxis, and navigate—download offline maps beforehand. Resorts and towns have internet; plan accordingly for remote travel days.

items.label: Plan Ahead in Peak Season
items.text: Fiji's dry season (May–October) brings heavy tourist traffic; book flights, ferries, and rental cars 1–2 weeks in advance. Wet season (November–April) sees cyclone disruptions and fewer services.
Industrial action and strikes occasionally disrupt bus and ferry services, especially in Suva. Check news or ask locals before traveling; have backup plans.

items.label: Getting Around Island Groups
items.text: The three main groups (Viti Levu, Vanua Levu, Taveuni) are well-connected by ferry and domestic flights. Smaller islands rely on speedboats or resort shuttle boats; arrange onward transport when booking.
